steps.title: Target unethical persuasion
steps.content: The claim is not merely that sophists taught students. It is that Aristophanes satirizes sophists as teaching students to win arguments without regard for what is morally good. The strongest quotation must therefore mention unjust or morally worse argument, not just education in general.

steps.title: Choose the unjust argument
steps.content: Choice A focuses on Socrates studying astronomy from a suspended basket, so it concerns odd intellectual methods rather than immoral persuasion. Choice B only shows Strepsiades urging his son to be taught. Choice C is a general question to a new customer about what to learn. Choice D names the worse style of argument, says it maintains what is unjust, and asks that it be taught by all means, so it directly illustrates persuasive technique separated from moral goodness. The answer is D.
